`start + end` can exceed the shorter string's length, causing `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` in the `substring` calls. For example, if `value="aa"` and `sanitized="a"`, then `start=1` and `end=1`, making `sanitized.substring(1, 0)` throw. Add a guard after both loops to clamp `end`.

Placeholder format `{0}` is inconsistent with all other `error-*` messages in this file, which use `{{0}}` (i18next interpolation). If the account-ui renders these client-side with i18next, the `{0}` placeholders and `{2,choice,...}` ChoiceFormat pattern will appear literally to the user instead of being interpolated.

`picocli.exit()` is the wrong exit-code path for the `metadata` command. Unlike the `check` command (which intentionally communicates results via distinct exit codes), this command never otherwise uses custom exit codes—every other error path throws `PropertyException`. Calling `picocli.exit()` here may bypass cleanup and is inconsistent with the error-handling pattern in this class. Consider throwing a `PropertyException` instead, which picocli's framework will translate to an appropriate error exit code.

qodo-extended.text: ClientPermissionsV2 queries resources using ownerId=server.getId(), but AdminPermissionsSchema creates resources owned by resourceServer.getClientId(), preventing resource lookup and breaking permission checks

getResourceTypeResource() can return null but the result is passed to findByResource() without null check, causing NullPointerException

qodo-extended-summary.text: ClientPermissionsV2 uses resourceServer.getId() as the ResourceStore ownerId when looking up resources, but resources are created/owned by resourceServer.getClientId(), so lookups fail and client/all-clients scope checks can incorrectly deny access

In ClientPermissionsV2.hasPermission(ClientModel, String), when no specific resource exists for the client, getResourceTypeResource() can return null which is passed directly to findByResource(server, resource) on line 219, causing a NullPointerException

The event listener in AdminPermissions.registerListener() wraps all cleanup logic in Profile.isFeatureEnabled(Profile.Feature.ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ) (V1), but ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2 does NOT depend on V1. When only V2 is enabled, the listener never fires, so client deletion leaves orphaned authorization resources in the admin permissions client's resource server

In ClientPermissionsV2.hasPermission(ClientModel, String), the loop over evaluated permissions does not filter by permission.getResourceId().equals(resource.getId()), unlike the equivalent UserPermissionsV2.hasPermission() method. This means that if the authorization evaluator returns permissions for multiple resources, a scope granted for one resource could incorrectly be accepted as a grant for a different resource
The private method getEvaluationContext(ClientModel, AccessToken) in ClientPermissionsV2 is defined but never called anywhere in the class, making it unreachable dead code

qodo.text: User search now relies on setting session attribute UserModel.GROUPS with auth.groups().getGroupIdsWithViewPermission() and no longer applies an explicit .filter(usersEvaluator::canView) on the returned stream. Need to validate that the underlying searchForUserStream(...) implementation actually enforces visibility based on UserModel.GROUPS in all storage/provider paths, and that an empty set does not accidentally broaden results for callers without global view permissions.

The code conditionally sets session.setAttribute(UserModel.GROUPS, groupIds) but does not clear/reset it after the search. Need to confirm that this session attribute is request-scoped (or otherwise safely isolated) so it cannot leak into subsequent operations on the same KeycloakSession, causing unintended filtering or authorization side effects.

hasPermission(...) uses resourceStore.findByName(server, groupId) for per-group checks but getGroupIdsWithViewPermission() iterates resources by type and passes groupResource.getId() back into hasPermission(...). Need to ensure the Resource name/ID conventions are consistent (group resource names vs ids) so permission checks don't silently fail or behave inconsistently across all-groups vs per-group resources.

The feature-flag guard only checks V1 (ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ), but V2 (ADMIN_FINE_GRAINED_AUTHZ_V2) can be enabled independently. When only V2 is active, this listener is never registered, so removing a role, client, or group won't clean up its associated permissions — they become orphaned.

Removing the !auth.users().canView() guard means that admins with full user-view permission will now have their user search narrowed to group members whenever getGroupIdsWithViewPermission() returns a non-empty set. Previously, full-view users bypassed group filtering entirely. This can cause admins to silently see fewer users than they're authorized to view.

`canView(group)` does not include the realm-wide `canView()` check, so flat group listings can become empty for admins who have global Groups VIEW/MANAGE but no per-group grants, dropping support for V2's global group-view permission.

cubic-dev.text: Adding a non-default method to a public interface is a breaking API change for external implementors. Provide a default implementation (e.g., returning `0`) to maintain backward compatibility, consistent with how `ProviderFactory.order()` is declared in this codebase.

The single P-256 key pair is reused for all three algorithm tests. `testES384` and `testES512` never exercise realistic P-384/P-521 signature component sizes because the R and S values from the P-256 key are far shorter than what those algorithms produce in practice. Generate algorithm-appropriate key pairs so the DER↔concatenatedRS conversion is tested with correctly-sized components.

`getBouncyCastleProvider()` returns the default JKS keystore provider (typically SUN), not a BouncyCastle provider. `KeyStore.getInstance(KeyStore.getDefaultType()).getProvider()` resolves to whichever provider backs the default keystore type, which is unrelated to BouncyCastle. If this simple provider intentionally has no BC dependency, consider returning `null` or throwing `UnsupportedOperationException` (like other unsupported methods) instead of returning a misleading provider instance.

Indefinite-length encoding (0x80) returns -1, but no caller handles this value. `readInteger()` will throw `NegativeArraySizeException`, `readSequence()` silently returns an empty list, and `readNext()` reads the wrong number of bytes. Since DER disallows indefinite-length, throw an `IOException` here instead of returning -1.

`debugf` treats its first argument as a format string. Since `builder` contains arbitrary class names (not a format pattern), use `logger.debug(builder.toString())` to avoid a potential `MissingFormatArgumentException` if a class name contains `%`.

These two `ASN1Encoder.create().write(...)` calls are dead code — they allocate encoders, write to their internal streams, and the results are immediately discarded. The actual encoding is done in the subsequent `return` statement. Remove these lines.
Trailing ", " after the last provider name in the log message. Consider using `String.join` or adding a conditional separator to avoid the dangling comma.
Non-standard modifier order: `static public` should be `public static` per Java conventions (JLS §8.4.3).

The crypto provider selection code sorts providers by order() and selects the one with the highest value. Tests lack verification that processes are alive and do not simulate crashes or hangs

entelligence.text: Breaking out of the loop when the deadline is exceeded means any remaining processes are never terminated, leaving orphaned workers alive after join()

sourcery.text: Metric tag keys for shard(s) are inconsistent across timers - 'produce' uses tags={"shard": shard_tag} while 'wait_produce' uses tags={"shards": shard_tag}, which complicates aggregation

Processes that haven't been waited on yet may never be terminated if the join deadline is exceeded early - when deadline expires, the break statement skips remaining processes in self.processes so their terminate() is never called
The added time.sleep(0.1) is a no-op because time.sleep is monkeypatched to lambda _: None earlier in the test, so it doesn't actually wait or help the flusher progress

When creating per-process SpansBuffer instances in _create_process_for_shards, only shards are passed, so if SpansBuffer relies on other configuration/state from the original buffer (e.g. Redis client, keys, limits), those settings are not preserved
In _ensure_processes_alive, the restart logic treats both processes and threads uniformly (checking exitcode, calling kill, etc.), but threads don't support those semantics

cubic-dev.text: `isinstance(process, multiprocessing.Process)` is always `False` for `SpawnProcess` objects created by `get_context("spawn")`. `SpawnProcess` inherits from `BaseProcess`, not `multiprocessing.Process`. This means unhealthy processes are never actually killed, and processes are never terminated during `join()`. Use `not isinstance(process, threading.Thread)` or `isinstance(process, multiprocessing.process.BaseProcess)` instead.

`time.sleep(0.1)` is a no-op here because `time.sleep` was already monkeypatched to `lambda _: None` at the top of this test. The flusher threads won't actually get any time to process. Save a reference to the real `sleep` before patching.
Inconsistent metric tag key: `"shards"` here vs `"shard"` used by the other two metrics in this method. This will cause the `wait_produce` metric to be grouped under a different tag in dashboards.

`break` on deadline exits the loop entirely, skipping `terminate()` for all remaining processes. Consider using `continue` to skip waiting but still call `terminate()`, or add a cleanup loop after the `break` to terminate remaining processes.
`_create_process_for_shard` (singular) is defined but never called anywhere. Only `_create_process_for_shards` (plural) is used. This is dead code that should be removed or integrated.

mesa.text: When `buffer.assigned_shards` is empty (e.g., during partition rebalancing), `self.num_processes` becomes 0, causing a ZeroDivisionError on line 65 when computing `i % self.num_processes`
The restart threshold check uses `>` before incrementing, which allows `MAX_PROCESS_RESTARTS + 1` total restarts instead of the intended cap, prolonging crash-loop thrashing
The backpressure logic rejects ALL ingestion when ANY single process exceeds the threshold, creating global throttling from a single shard-group hotspot and potentially over-throttling the system
Killed processes are not properly joined/closed before replacement, risking resource leaks in long-running deployments with frequent restarts
Using comma-joined shard lists as metric tags introduces unbounded cardinality that scales with partition topology changes, creating observability cost and complexity problems

codeant.text: Unrelated/accidental doc change included in CLAUDE.md about hasattr vs isinstance examples that should be removed from the PR
CLI default for --flusher-processes is set to 1, reducing parallelism from previous behavior of one process per shard; consider making default None or 0 to maintain auto-sizing behavior

process_restarts counter is never reset, so infrequent flaps over time could accumulate and force consumer restart even if individual flaps are rare; consider resetting counter after process stays healthy for some time
Tests use threading.Thread while production uses multiprocessing.Process with multiprocessing.Value objects, which have different concurrency semantics and may behave differently
Tests use time.sleep to allow background processing, which is fragile and timing-dependent; consider using synchronization primitives or polling conditions instead
Health metrics could include process index tag in addition to shard tag to make it easier to map metrics to processes in dashboards

kodus.text: A `None` default for an integer parameter causes a likely `TypeError`. The `__init__` method adds `flusher_processes` with a default of `None`. However, existing callers like the `test_basic` test do not pass this new argument, causing `self.flusher_processes` to be `None`. This `None` value is then passed to `SpanFlusher` as `max_processes`, which expects an integer for process limit calculations, leading to a `TypeError`. The production consumer configuration specifies a default of `1`, indicating `None` is not an expected value.

Orphaned process leak during shutdown. The `join` method's timeout logic contains a `break` statement that prematurely exits the process cleanup loop. If the timeout expires while waiting for one process, all subsequent processes are abandoned and left running as orphans, leaking system resources.

Redundant Redis queries for memory check. The `submit` method iterates through all per-process `SpansBuffer` instances and calls `get_memory_info()` on each. If multiple processes handle shards on the same Redis node, this sends multiple, identical `INFO` commands to that node, creating unnecessary network I/O and load on the Redis server.

Ineffective `time.sleep` call creates a race condition. The added `time.sleep(0.1)` is intended to allow background flusher processes time to work, but it has no effect because `time.sleep` is patched to a no-op at the beginning of the test function on line 15. This creates a race condition and will lead to a flaky test that fails intermittently depending on process scheduling.

Dead synchronization: `time.sleep(0.1)` on line 62 is patched to a no-op by `monkeypatch.setattr("time.sleep", lambda _: None)` on line 15. The comment states this sleep is needed to give flusher threads time to process after the drift change, but it executes instantly, providing zero synchronization. `step.join()` is called immediately after a no-op sleep, causing a race where the flusher thread may not have produced the message yet — `(msg,) = messages` then raises `ValueError: not enough values to unpack`.

claude-code.text: isinstance(process, multiprocessing.Process) is always False for SpawnProcess objects created via get_context("spawn").Process(), since SpawnProcess inherits from BaseProcess, not multiprocessing.Process. This means process.kill() in _ensure_processes_alive (line 254) and process.terminate() in join() (line 347) are never called
The kill() call being skipped is a regression since the pre-PR code called it unconditionally, potentially leaving zombie processes consuming resources
The break at line 340 exits the entire for loop when the deadline expires, so terminate() is skipped for all remaining processes even if the isinstance bug were fixed
_create_process_for_shard (singular, line 127) is defined but never called anywhere - dead code that should be removed
Having both _create_process_for_shard and _create_process_for_shards methods with near-identical names is confusing for future maintainers

Typo on line 199: tags={"shards": shard_tag} should be tags={"shard": shard_tag} (singular) to match the other two metrics on lines 186 and 195, causing dashboards filtering by the shard tag to silently miss the wait_produce timer

qodo-v2-2.text: SpanFlusher accepts negative --flusher-processes values which causes an empty process_to_shards_map and crashes during initialization with a KeyError
SpanFlusher uses 'max_processes or len(...)' which conflates None and 0 values
Missing explicit validation in strategy factory or SpanFlusher.__init__ to reject max_processes < 1
CLI option --flusher-processes accepts any integer without validation

qodo-v22.text: SpanFlusher creates a SpansBuffer per worker containing only that worker's shard subset, so SpansBuffer.flush_segments computes max_segments_per_shard from the subset size and can flush up to spans.buffer.max-flush-segments per process rather than per consumer. This breaks the global flush cap and weakens the backpressure signal (any_shard_at_limit), potentially overproducing to Kafka and increasing Redis churn.

SpanFlusher.join stops iterating over flusher processes when the timeout deadline is exceeded, so remaining processes are neither waited for nor terminated. This can leave child flushers running after shutdown/rebalance, leaking resources and potentially producing unexpectedly.

--flusher-processes accepts negative integers and SpanFlusher uses the value to size process_to_shards_map and compute i % self.num_processes, which then indexes a dict that has no corresponding keys. The consumer can crash on startup with a bad CLI value.

In SpanFlusher.main, the 'produce' timer and 'segment_size_bytes' use tag key 'shard' while 'wait_produce' uses 'shards' for the same shard_tag value. This splits metrics by tag schema and prevents consistent per-shard querying across these flusher timings.

qodo-extended.text: hasattr(x,"replace") union narrowing in CLAUDE.md uses hasattr() instead of isinstance() to narrow a str | None union, which conflicts with required isinstance()-based narrowing guidance
Negative process count crash: --flusher-processes accepts negative values which causes SpanFlusher to build invalid process_to_shards_map and crash during shard assignment with KeyError

Flush limit multiplied: SpansBuffer.flush_segments computes max_segments_per_shard using smaller per-process shard count, causing spans.buffer.max-flush-segments limit to be effectively applied per process and exceeded by up to num_processes× per flush cycle

Thread restart leaks: When produce_to_pipe is set, _ensure_processes_alive only kills multiprocessing.Process instances but not threads, allowing unhealthy threads to continue running when replaced, causing duplicate produces and resource leaks

join() skips terminating remaining processes: When deadline expires during iteration over processes, the break statement exits loop without calling terminate() on remaining processes, leaving zombie flusher processes running that continue accessing Redis shards

cubic-v2.text: `isinstance(process, multiprocessing.Process)` is always `False` for processes created via `multiprocessing.get_context("spawn")`, because `SpawnProcess` does not inherit from `multiprocessing.Process` (they are sibling subclasses of `BaseProcess`). This means unhealthy processes are never killed before being restarted, and processes are never terminated during `join()`. Use `multiprocessing.process.BaseProcess` or just check `not isinstance(process, threading.Thread)` instead.
`default=1` forces the flusher back to a single process. Leave this unset so the existing `SpanFlusher` default can scale to the assigned shard count.
This delay is a no-op because `test_basic` already monkeypatches `time.sleep` to `lambda _: None`, so it does not actually give the flusher thread time to run.

Inconsistent metric tag key: `"shards"` here vs `"shard"` used on the `flusher.produce` and `segment_size_bytes` metrics above. This will split metrics across two different tag keys in your monitoring system.

When the deadline is exceeded, `break` exits the loop and skips calling `terminate()` on the remaining flusher processes. Those processes will keep running (potentially flushing stale data) until the parent process exits. Consider terminating all remaining processes before breaking, or restructuring so that the terminate block runs unconditionally for every process.

qodo-extended-summary.text: SpanFlusher accepts max_processes without validating it is a positive integer; negative values can break shard→process mapping at startup (i % self.num_processes raises ZeroDivisionError)
CLI option --flusher-processes is declared as type=int with no min bound, allowing negative values like --flusher-processes=-1
In thread mode (produce_to_pipe set), unhealthy worker restarts spawn new threads without stopping old ones, causing duplicate flushing and unbounded thread leaks
_ensure_processes_alive only kills multiprocessing.Process instances but not threading.Thread instances during restart
In join(), when deadline expires during process iteration, break exits the loop without terminating remaining processes, leaving them running after shutdown
Metric tag key inconsistency: spans.buffer.flusher.wait_produce uses 'shards' (plural) while other metrics use 'shard' (singular)

sourcery.text: Mutual-exclusion validation for `age`/`timestamp` is based on truthiness and will miss zero values. `validate_timestamp` and `validate_age` rely on `self.initial_data.get(...)` in a truthiness check, so values like `0` or `""` won't trigger the mutual-exclusion error. If the rule is based on presence/absence, check for key presence or `is not None` instead, e.g. `if "age" in self.initial_data` or `if self.initial_data.get("age") is not None`, and similarly for `timestamp`.

Feature-flagged `TableWidgetVisualization` path ignores actual table results and always renders an empty table. In the feature-flagged branch, `TableWidgetVisualization` always receives empty `columns` and `tableData`, so flag-enabled users will see an empty table even when `tableResults` is populated. To keep behavior consistent with `StyledSimpleTableChart`, pass through the same `fields`, `metadata`, `data`, and any custom field renderers into `TableWidgetVisualization` before applying the feature flag.

In `CommitContextIntegration.get_merged_pr_single_issue_template`, the static method references `PRCommentWorkflow._truncate_title`, but `_truncate_title` is defined on `CommitContextIntegration`; this will raise at runtime—use the correct class (`CommitContextIntegration._truncate_title` or refactor to a classmethod).

In `FeedbackListPage`, `FeedbackSummary` is rendered inside `SummaryListContainer` but does not appear to be imported in this file; ensure `FeedbackSummary` is imported from `sentry/components/feedback/feedbackSummary` to avoid a runtime/TS error.

cubic-dev.text: Hidden attributes are only filtered when the user is actively searching. When `searchQuery` is empty, the early return on the previous line returns `sorted` without filtering out `HIDDEN_ATTRIBUTES`, so `is_segment`, `project_id`, and `received` will still be visible by default.

`zip(error_ids, events.values())` assumes the dict returned by `get_multi` preserves the input order of `node_ids`, but `get_multi` reorders entries when some are served from cache (uncached items first, then `items.update(cache_items)` appends cached ones). This silently pairs error IDs with wrong event data.

`TableWidgetVisualization` is rendered with hardcoded empty `columns` and `tableData`, discarding the actual query results (`result.data`, `result.meta`, `fields`) that are available in scope. Anyone enabling the `use-table-widget-visualization` feature flag will see blank tables.

`projectIds` is a new array reference every render (when `projects` is provided), defeating the downstream `useMemo`/`useCallback` dependency checks and causing unnecessary query refetches. Wrap it in `useMemo`.

Truthiness check on `self.initial_data.get("age")` fails for falsy values like `0`. Since `age` and `timestamp` are integers, a value of `0` is falsy in Python and would bypass this mutual exclusivity validation. Use `"age" in self.initial_data` to check for key presence instead.

Same truthiness bug as in `validate_timestamp`: `self.initial_data.get("timestamp")` returns `0` (falsy) when `timestamp=0`, which is a valid value per `min_value=0`. Use `"timestamp" in self.initial_data` instead.

`processing_deadline_duration=120` (2 min) is shorter than the task's internal `BATCH_SCHEDULE_OFFSET` deadline of 180 seconds (3 min). The task will be killed by the worker before it can exit gracefully via its own deadline check, potentially interrupting mid-delivery. This should be at least 180 seconds (or ideally match `drain_mailbox`'s 300-second buffer).

The OAuth `state` parameter is set to `pipeline.signature`, which appears to be a deterministic pipeline-derived value rather than a per-request cryptographic random nonce. RFC 6749 §10.12 recommends the `state` be an unpredictable per-request value bound to the user's session to effectively prevent CSRF during OAuth flows.

`integration.metadata["sender"]` will raise a `KeyError` if the `sender` key was never populated. In `build_integration()`, `sender` is only added to metadata when `state.get("sender")` is truthy. Use `.get()` with a safe fallback to avoid crashing the installation flow.

Using `transparent` (which is `rgba(0,0,0,0)`) instead of a zero-alpha version of the background color causes a dark band in the gradient when the theme background isn't black. The `LeftMask` already handles this correctly with `Color(p.theme.background).alpha(0).rgb().string()` — the `RightMask` should match.

`summary` can be `null` (per the hook's return type), but the component doesn't guard against it. If the API returns a successful response with a `null` summary, the UI will render the "Feedback Summary" header and icon with an empty content area. Add a null/empty check before rendering.

Replacing `FlexCenter` with `Flex` drops the `overflow: hidden` (from `overflowEllipsis`) that the old styled component had. Because this element is a CSS-grid item, it needs `overflow: hidden` or `min-width: 0` to allow the cell to shrink below its content width — otherwise long file names will overflow instead of showing an ellipsis.

The migration from `FlexBox` to `Flex` drops `height: 100%`. The old styled component explicitly set `height: 100%`, which is not replicated by any prop on the `Flex` component. This could cause layout regression in contexts where the parent doesn't stretch children via flex (e.g., the replay detail view). Consider adding `style={{height: '100%'}}` or wrapping with an additional style prop.

The `useCallback` dependency includes the entire `selection` object, but only `selection.datetime` and `selection.projects` are used in the callback body. This causes unnecessary re-creation of the callback (and downstream re-renders) whenever unrelated page filter state like `environments` changes. The sibling hook `useGetTraceItemAttributeValues` correctly lists `selection.projects` and `selection.datetime` separately. Also, `organization` can be narrowed to `organization.slug` for the same reason.

Analytics event is recorded before the feature flag check, so requests that are rejected due to the feature being disabled will still be tracked. Move `analytics.record()` after the `features.has()` guard to avoid polluting analytics data with rejected requests.
Missing `align="center"` and `gap={space(1)}` — the old `FlexBox` had both. The three placeholder items will lose their vertical centering and spacing.

Missing `align="center"` and `gap={space(1)}` — the old `Container` inherited these from `FlexBox`. This will cause a visual regression (tabs and vitals won't be vertically centered and will lose their gap).

`name` is typed as `TFields` but represents a display label, not a field key. When `TabularColumn` is instantiated with a specific field union (e.g., `TabularColumn<'count' | 'avg'>`), `name` is restricted to those literal keys, preventing friendly display names like `"Average"`. Consider typing `name` as `string`.

`SummaryListContainer` is missing `min-height: 0` (or `overflow: hidden`). As a CSS Grid item in a `1fr` row, it defaults to `min-height: auto`, which prevents it from shrinking below its content. This breaks the scroll behavior of the inner `FeedbackList` — content will overflow the grid cell instead of scrolling.

The `_handle_regex_match` inner function in `parametrize_w_experiments` is now dead code — its only call site was the removed `else` branch. It should be removed to avoid confusion (note: the identically-named function in `parametrize_w_regex` is still used).
The new subscription results consumers are missing from the `full` mode. The `full` mode includes every other consumer defined in the file; omitting these four new consumers looks like an oversight.

`Boolean(normalizedDateRange)` is always `true` because `normalizeDateTimeParams` returns an object (even an empty `{}`). This guard is a no-op — the query will fire whenever the feature flag is present, regardless of whether valid date params exist. Check for non-empty keys instead.

Truthy check on `cell` conflates valid falsy `ReactNode` values (`null`, `0`, `""`, `false`) with `undefined`. The return type `React.ReactNode | undefined` uses `undefined` to mean "use default", so the check should be `cell !== undefined`. Same issue applies to `renderDefaultBodyCell` below.

`format_comment_subtitle` is now dead code. The only call site was removed in this change (replaced by `self.get_merged_pr_single_issue_template` which doesn't use subtitle). Consider removing the method.

Inconsistent error response format between the two 422 paths: the non-list-data case returns an empty `Response(status=422)` while the validation-failure case returns a JSON body `{"error": ..., "details": ...}`. Both should use the same format. This also changes behavior from the previous `HttpResponse(status=422)` which always had an empty body, potentially breaking clients that parse these responses.
`attributes` can be undefined on initial load because `data` (and `previous`) are undefined until the query resolves. Provide a TagCollection fallback to keep the return shape stable.

`hidden-xs hidden-sm` is on the inner `TimeSince` but the wrapping `TextOverflow` div will still render as an empty flex item on small screens. Move the `className` to `TextOverflow` so the entire wrapper is hidden.

`pipeline.fetch_state("github_authenticated_user")` can return `None` if the OAuth login step didn't execute properly. This would cause the comparison to always evaluate as a mismatch (`None != <login>`), returning a confusing `USER_MISMATCH` error instead of handling the missing state explicitly.
Typo: "segements" should be "segments".

`dedupedYAxes` is a misleading name — it's now just `[chart.yAxis]`, a single-element array with no deduplication. Consider simplifying this block to process `chart.yAxis` directly and renaming the variable to remove the stale "deduped" terminology.

cubic-dev.text: Django QuerySets do not support negative indexing — `queryset[negative_offset:stop]` raises `AssertionError: Negative indexing is not supported.` The comment claiming Django handles this automatically is incorrect. Misleading comments claim negative offsets are now supported as a "performance optimization" but no code was actually changed. The existing pagination logic (`_build_next_values`, `_build_prev_values`) does not handle negative offsets correctly — e.g., `offset + limit` would produce wrong page boundaries, and `bool(value or offset)` would incorrectly signal a previous page exists.

`OptimizedCursorPaginator.get_item_key()` applies `math.floor()`/`math.ceil()` directly to the raw attribute value. With `order_by="-datetime"`, this value is a `datetime` object, which will raise `TypeError`. The existing `DateTimePaginator` avoids this by converting to a float timestamp first via `strftime("%s.%f")`.

Passing `enable_advanced_features=True` enables the negative-offset code path in `OptimizedCursorPaginator.get_result()`, where `queryset[negative_offset:stop]` is called. Django querysets do not support negative indexing and will raise `AssertionError: Negative indexing is not supported`.

`organization_context.member` can be `None` (per its type annotation and docstring in `RpcUserOrganizationContext`). Accessing `.has_global_access` on `None` will raise an `AttributeError` at runtime for users without org membership.

`math.floor`/`math.ceil` will raise `TypeError` if the sort key is a `datetime` field (e.g., audit log timestamps). Unlike `DateTimePaginator`, this class doesn't convert the value to a float first via `strftime`. If this paginator is meant for audit logs, it needs datetime handling similar to `DateTimePaginator.get_item_key`.

`OptimizedCursorPaginator` is a newly introduced class with no other references in the codebase. It contains fundamental issues (datetime handling, negative slicing) that indicate it hasn't been adequately tested. Consider using the established `DateTimePaginator` or thoroughly vetting this new paginator before use.

cubic-dev.text: `TotalDocs` iterates `b.cache` without acquiring `b.cacheMu.RLock()`. Since `BuildIndex` now writes to the map under the lock, any concurrent call to `TotalDocs` during index building will trigger Go's `fatal error: concurrent map iteration and map write` runtime panic. Add `b.cacheMu.RLock()`/`b.cacheMu.RUnlock()` around the iteration.

Narrowing the lock to only the cache-write removes the mutual exclusion that prevented concurrent builds for the same key. Two goroutines calling `BuildIndex` with identical `key` will both create and populate a bleve index in parallel. For file-backed indices this calls `bleve.New` on the same directory twice, which will fail or corrupt. For memory indices the loser's index is silently leaked (never closed). Consider using a per-key lock (e.g., `sync.Map` of `sync.Mutex` per key or `singleflight`) so that concurrent builds of the *same* key are serialized while different keys can still proceed in parallel.

copilot.text: When an exception occurs during message processing in the OrderedQueueWorker, the offset is still marked as complete in the finally block. This means failed messages will have their offsets committed, potentially causing data loss.

If the commit_function call on line 287 succeeds but any of the mark_committed calls on line 289 fail, the offsets will be committed to Kafka but not marked as committed in the local tracker. This creates an inconsistency where the tracker will attempt to commit these offsets again on the next iteration.

The _get_partition_lock method has a race condition. If two threads call this method simultaneously for the same partition that doesn't have a lock yet, both will pass the initial check and could create separate locks via setdefault, breaking the thread-safety guarantee.

The exception handler in the submit method has a logic issue. When queue_pool.submit is called on line 315, it internally adds the offset to the tracker. If an exception occurs during or after the queue_pool.submit call, the except block will add the offset again and immediately mark it complete. However, if the item was successfully queued before the exception, this creates a race where the offset could be committed before the item is actually processed by the worker.

The OrderedQueueWorker's run method calls queue.get() without a timeout. This means the thread will block indefinitely if the queue is empty, preventing graceful shutdown when self.shutdown is set to True.

The _commit_loop waits first and then checks for committable offsets. This means that on the first iteration, the thread will wait for 1 second before processing any commits, introducing unnecessary latency.

The queue pool uses unbounded queue.Queue() instances and submit() always enqueues, but the strategy docstring claims 'Natural backpressure when queues fill up'. Under slow processing this can grow memory without bound and increase end-to-end latency.

Offsets are committed from a dedicated background thread rather than from poll()/join() like other strategies in this repo. This increases the risk of race conditions with lifecycle events (shutdown/partition revocation) and makes commit behavior harder to reason about.

OffsetTracker.get_committable_offsets scans range(start, max_offset + 1) and keeps all in-flight offsets in sets. In worst cases with large gaps/high in-flight windows, this can become CPU/memory heavy and run every second in the commit loop.

sourcery.text: The broad exception handling and truthiness check on client_sample_rate may skip valid values and hide errors. Since chained `.get()` calls on dicts won't usually raise, the broad `except Exception` implies you're guarding against `contexts` not being a mapping. If that's needed, prefer an explicit `isinstance(contexts, Mapping)` check rather than catching all exceptions, so real errors aren't masked. The `if client_sample_rate:` check will skip valid values like `0` or "0", even though `float("0")` is valid. If a zero sample rate should be allowed, check `client_sample_rate is not None` and rely on `float()` to validate instead.

Using Python's hash() in the cache key may be unstable and can leave stale entries across process restarts. Using `hash(tuple(sorted(project_ids)))` makes the key process-specific due to Python's hash randomization and also vulnerable to collisions. After a restart, the same project set can map to a different key, leaving stale entries and forcing `invalidate_upsampling_cache` to replicate this exact scheme to work correctly. Use a deterministic representation instead (e.g. `','.join(map(str, sorted(project_ids)))` or a stable hash via `hashlib.md5`) so keys are consistent across processes and reliably targetable by the invalidation logic.

A simple substring match on `event.type:error` can misclassify more complex or negated queries. This substring check will also match `!event.type:error`, `event.type:transaction OR event.type:error`, or occurrences inside quoted strings, so the upsampling transform may be enabled for mixed or explicitly non-error queries. For a more reliable check, consider using the existing search parser (or a lightweight variant) to inspect `event.type` filters structurally instead of by substring. If that's too heavy for now, you could at least tighten the heuristic (e.g., tokenizing on whitespace and AND/OR) to avoid obvious negation cases.

Missing test that exercises the topEvents path with error upsampling enabled. The new tests cover the basic timeseries path, but `_get_event_stats` has a separate `top_events > 0` branch that also calls `transform_query_columns_for_error_upsampling` and isn't exercised.

Missing tests for `is_errors_query_for_error_upsampled_projects` including its caching behavior. Unit tests currently cover the helpers, but the main entry point's behavior (cache key construction, cache get/set, and its conjunction with `_should_apply_sample_weight_transform`) isn't exercised.

Missing test for `invalidate_upsampling_cache` to validate correct cache key computation. Right now only `_are_all_projects_error_upsampled` is covered; `invalidate_upsampling_cache` isn't. Since it must stay in sync with `is_errors_query_for_error_upsampled_projects` on the cache key format, a regression in either could leave stale cache entries.

Current tests for `transform_query_columns_for_error_upsampling` don't clarify expected behavior for more complex `count()` expressions. Current tests cover basic `count()` forms, case insensitivity, and whitespace. To better document and lock in expected behavior, consider adding a test with a more complex expression (e.g. `"count() as other"` or `"count_if(condition)"`) to show these are intentionally left unchanged.

entelligence.text: Cache key generated by `userPermDenialCacheKey` overlaps with keyspace of `userPermCacheKey`, causing collisions when action contains underscores (e.g., action='read_name_parent' vs action='read' with name='name'). Need unique prefix like `.perm_denial_` for keyspace isolation

cubic-dev.text: The `Check` method trusts cached grants (returns `allowed=true` immediately) but ignores cached denials (falls through to re-query the DB when `allowed=false`). This creates a security asymmetry: newly revoked permissions continue to be honored for up to the cache TTL (30s), while newly granted permissions are picked up immediately via re-query.

Test value should be `true`, not `false`, to actually verify the denial cache takes precedence. The comment says 'Allow access to the dashboard to prove this is not checked', but `false` means the permission cache also denies access, so the test would pass even if the denial cache logic were removed.

The `permDenialCache` stores `true` to represent 'access denied,' which is semantically confusing — `true` conventionally implies an affirmative/allow result. Since the stored value is discarded at the `Get` call site (only key existence matters), consider using a more self-documenting sentinel value or adding a comment clarifying that the stored boolean value is irrelevant.
`NoopCache` is exported but only used within this package. Consider making it unexported (`noopCache`) to reduce the public API surface.
Receiver name `lc` is misleading for `NoopCache` — it was copied from `LocalCache`. Consider renaming to `nc` (or `_` since it's unused).

mesa.text: Cache key construction uses simple string concatenation with underscore delimiters, creating collision risks when fields contain underscores (e.g., 'a_b' + 'c' vs 'a' + 'b_c')
Empty values in cache key fields compound collision issues
When cachedPerms contains permissions but specific resource check fails, code falls through to query database again, defeating cache purpose for negative checks
DB fallback occurs when cache has data but specific resource permission is missing, instead of only when cache returns ErrNotFound
Denial cache check happens before evaluating cached permissions, causing users to remain denied even after gaining permissions until TTL expires
Denial cache lacks mutation-triggered invalidation, creating lingering denials after permission grants or resource moves
Denied permissions trigger DB queries on every check until first denial is cached, potentially spiking database load during authorization failures

kodus.text: Cache key collision in userPermDenialCacheKey: using underscore separator allows different (name, parent) combinations to produce identical keys (e.g., name='A' parent='B_C' vs name='A_B' parent='C'), causing incorrect authorization denials

claude-code.text: The denial cache key (userPermDenialCacheKey) does not include the identity type, causing anonymous:0 and render:0 to produce identical cache keys and enabling cross-identity cache poisoning where anonymous user denials block render service access

The denial cache key uses raw checkReq.UserUID instead of the resolved userIdentifiers.UID (as getCachedIdentityPermissions does), reducing cache effectiveness when users are identified by numeric ID vs string UID across requests

kodus-v2.text: Asymmetric cache-first logic between Check and List methods: Check falls through to DB when cached permissions don't grant access, but List uses cached permissions directly without fallthrough, causing List to return incomplete results when cache is stale while Check self-corrects

qodo-v2-2.text: userPermDenialCacheKey concatenates userUID/action/name/parent using '_' separators even though Grafana UIDs may contain '_', causing cache key collisions where different (name,parent) tuples generate the same cache key and incorrectly reuse a cached denial for the wrong resource/folder

qodo-v22.text: Service.Check returns immediately on a permDenialCache hit, so a previously cached denial will override the new 'requery DB on stale permissions' behavior and can keep denying for the cache TTL even after the DB would allow. This can reintroduce the exact post-change access issue (permission changes from deny→allow) for up to ~30s.

userPermDenialCacheKey concatenates action/name/parent with '_' without escaping or length-prefixing, so different (name,parent) tuples can generate identical cache keys. This can apply a cached denial to the wrong resource and incorrectly deny access.

permDenialCache entries are keyed by (user, action, name, parent) where name and parent come directly from the request, so distinct denied resource names/folders produce distinct cache entries. This can create high-cardinality cache churn and memory/eviction pressure under large resource sets or adversarial traffic patterns.

The denial cache key uses checkReq.UserUID parsed from the subject string, but permission caching uses the canonical userIdentifiers.UID resolved via GetUserIdentifiers (which normalizes numeric IDs). This mismatch can produce multiple denial keys for the same user identity (e.g., 'user:123' vs 'user:<uid>') and reduces cache consistency/effectiveness.

qodo-extended.text: Service.Check short-circuits to Allowed=false when the denial cache key exists, so a previously denied (namespace,user,action,name,folder) tuple will stay denied until the cache TTL expires even if the user gains permission in the meantime
Service.List returns the cached permission set on cache hit and never verifies it against the DB, so if the cache is outdated it can omit newly created/granted resources until TTL expiry
userPermDenialCacheKey function joins name and parent with underscore delimiters, but dashboard/folder UIDs can contain underscores, making field boundaries ambiguous and causing cache key collisions
When getCachedIdentityPermissions succeeds but checkPermission returns an error, the function returns without incrementing the permissionCacheUsage metric
When cache hits but permission is denied (allowed=false), the metric incorrectly records it as a cache miss, conflating cache misses with cache-hit-but-denied scenarios
Attacker can bypass the denial cache by requesting random or non-existent resource names, forcing a database query on every request and potentially causing DoS and memory exhaustion

cubic-v2.text: `Check` trusts the denial cache (returns denied immediately) but deliberately ignores denials from the permission cache (re-queries DB to pick up new grants). This is contradictory: once a denial is stored in `permDenialCache` after a DB re-query, any permission granted afterward is blocked for up to 30s — the exact staleness problem the re-query path was designed to solve. Consider either not caching denials for the `Check` path, or invalidating the denial cache entry when the permission cache is refreshed.
Escape or encode `name` and `parent` in the denial-cache key; the current `_`-joined format can collide and cause false permission denials.
This test seeds the cached permission as denied, so it can pass even if `permDenialCache` is never consulted.

qodo-extended-summary.text: Service.Check consults permDenialCache before re-reading permissions and returns an immediate deny; if a user later gains permission (e.g., by creating the resource), they can still be denied until the cache TTL expires, contradicting the PR goal of allowing creators to access newly created dashboards/folders immediately

userPermDenialCacheKey function uses underscore as delimiter between action, name, and parent fields, but these fields can contain underscores, causing key collisions where different resources produce identical cache keys

Denial cache key is built using checkReq.UserUID (raw UID from subject string) while permission cache key uses userIdentifiers.UID (resolved canonical UID from DB), making denial cache ineffective for numeric-ID subjects
Service.Check treats any successful permDenialCache lookup as a deny regardless of the stored boolean value, making cache behavior dependent on key existence rather than value

When getCachedIdentityPermissions returns a cache hit but checkPermission returns allowed=false, the code increments permissionCacheUsage with 'false' (cache miss), understating the cache hit rate metric

Any error from getCachedIdentityPermissions (including real DB errors from GetUserIdentifiers) is silently treated as a cache miss and falls through to getIdentityPermissions, which calls GetUserIdentifiers again, doubling DB load during failures without logging the error

entelligence.text: `integration.metadata["sender"]["login"]` can raise `KeyError` for legacy integrations or installs without sender metadata, causing a 500 instead of a clean error. Use `.get()` with a safe default so missing metadata results in the intended error response.

Duplicate code detected (95% similarity) in `get_document_origin` function across `src/sentry/integrations/github/integration.py:377-383`, `408-414`, and `pipeline.py:240-242`. Should be extracted to a shared helper function.

sourcery.text: Catching bare `Exception` when parsing the OAuth token response may hide unexpected failures. The `except Exception:` around `safe_urlread(...).decode(...)` and `parse_qsl` turns any unexpected error (including programming or network issues) into a generic "invalid installation" path. Should narrow the except to specific parsing/IO errors or at least log unexpected exceptions.

Direct indexing into `integration.metadata["sender"]["login"]` risks a runtime error if metadata is missing or malformed. Should use safer lookup like `integration.metadata.get("sender", {}).get("login")` and treat missing value as invalid installation.
Inconsistent capitalization of "GitHub" in the short error message. `error_short` uses `"Github"` but other messages use `"GitHub"`.

Hard-coding the `responses.calls` index (e.g., `responses.calls[2]`) is brittle with the new OAuth requests. Should iterate over `responses.calls` and select the one whose `request.url` and/or method matches the installations access-token endpoint.

Unconditionally calling `pipeline.fetch_state` when reading `installation_id` from the query string can raise if the state isn't set. Should retrieve the query param first and only call `fetch_state` when it's missing.

In `GitHubInstallation.dispatch`, the new `Integration.objects.get(external_id=installation_id, status=ObjectStatus.ACTIVE)` block will cause fresh installations (where the webhook hasn't yet created the Integration row) to be treated as an invalid installation request. Should consider falling back to `pipeline.next_step()` when the integration does not yet exist.

The new `error()` helper returns a generic 'Invalid installation request.' message for several distinct failure modes (no integration, user mismatch, token/state failures). Should consider differentiating the error_short/error_long messages to make troubleshooting easier.
The new helper named `error` is very generic and shadows Python's built-in `Exception.error` idiom. Should consider renaming it to something domain-specific like `render_installation_error`.

The OAuth token exchange and parsing logic in `OAuthLoginView.dispatch` largely duplicates what exists in `OAuth2CallbackView`. Should consider refactoring into a shared helper so that future changes to the OAuth flow don't need to be updated in multiple places.

cubic-dev.text: The OAuth `state` parameter is set to `pipeline.signature`, which is a static MD5 hash derived from pipeline view class names — it's identical across all users and sessions. This defeats the CSRF protection that the `state` parameter is supposed to provide (RFC 6749 §10.12). Use a cryptographically random, per-session nonce instead (e.g., store a random token in the pipeline state and compare on callback).

`integration.metadata["sender"]["login"]` will raise a `KeyError` if the integration was created without a `sender` in its metadata. The `sender` key is only conditionally set in `build_integration` (`if state.get("sender")`), and `installation.py:44` already has a defensive check for this. Use `.get()` to handle the missing key gracefully.

`test_installation_not_found` no longer tests what it claims. The `init_path` call is missing (so no pipeline is initialized), the steps are in the wrong order (`installation_id` before `code`/`state`), and the state value doesn't match the one the pipeline would generate. The test passes with "Invalid installation request" due to a pipeline/state mismatch rather than because the installation was not found (HTTP 404).

`pipeline.fetch_state("github_authenticated_user")` returns `None` when the key isn't set (e.g., session expired between pipeline steps, or `OAuthLoginView` was somehow skipped). The `None != "somelogin"` comparison won't crash, but the code should explicitly check for a missing state and return a clear error before reaching the comparison, rather than relying on the implicit `None != string` behavior.

mesa.text: Line assumes both `sender` and `login` keys exist in `integration.metadata`. If webhook metadata is missing, corrupted, or from a legacy format, this will raise a KeyError and return a 500 error instead of failing gracefully. Should use defensive access: `integration.metadata.get("sender", {}).get("login")` and route to generic installation error when absent.

The `installation_id` is re-read from query params even after being stored in pipeline state, allowing parameter tampering if an attacker modifies the callback URL. Should prefer the pipeline state value once OAuth flow has started, or explicitly validate that query param matches the stored state value.
Missing test coverage for critical OAuth failure scenarios: invalid/missing `code` parameter, `error=access_denied` responses, malformed GitHub API responses, and state parameter mismatches.
The `error()` helper function has an overly generic name that could cause ambiguity. Should rename to `render_installation_error()` for improved code clarity.

cubic-v2.text: `integration.metadata["sender"]` raises `KeyError` when the integration was built without a `sender` in state (the key is set conditionally in `build_integration`). Use `.get()` with a safe default, e.g., `integration.metadata.get("sender", {}).get("login")`.

The OAuth `state` parameter is set to `pipeline.signature`, which is a deterministic MD5 hash of pipeline identifiers — not a per-request random value. OAuth 2.0 requires `state` to be an unpredictable, per-request nonce to prevent CSRF. Consider generating a cryptographically random token and storing it in the pipeline state for verification on callback.

`pipeline.fetch_state("github_authenticated_user")` can return `None` if the OAuth step was skipped or state was lost. Add an explicit `None` check before the comparison so the code fails clearly rather than relying on `None != <string>` semantics.
Test uses an invalid OAuth state, so the test never reaches the installation 404 path it is meant to cover.
The `redirect_uri` should be URL-encoded before being included in the query string. Use `urllib.parse.quote()` to properly encode the URL.

propel.text: The goroutine that replicates the write to the legacy store inherits the original request context (ctx). As soon as the REST handler returns, the apiserver cancels that context, so context.WithTimeoutCause(ctx, …) observes an already cancelled parent and returns immediately. In practice the asynchronous legacy Create (and the identical goroutines in Delete, Update, and DeleteCollection) never actually execute, which means Mode 3 silently degrades to 'write only to unified storage'. The context should be detached before spawning the goroutine (e.g. ctxLegacy := context.WithoutCancel(ctx) or context.Background() plus any required values) so the legacy write can run even after the request completes.

DeleteCollection records the legacy operation time via recordStorageDuration instead of recordLegacyDuration, causing all metrics emitted from the background legacy delete to be misclassified as storage operations rather than legacy operations. This misclassifies errors and latency.

The metrics call at line 106 uses name as the label argument instead of the resource kind (options.Kind). This will emit high-cardinality metric series per object name and misalign with the rest of the dashboard.

bugbot.text: applyTemplateVariables is called twice on the same queries when shard splitting is used with ad-hoc filters: first in runShardSplitQuery at the start, then again in runSplitQuery when shard splitting logic delegates to it (line 163), causing ad-hoc filters to be applied twice via addAdHocFilters and resulting in duplicate labels in the final query expression like {job="test", namespace="default", namespace="default"}

copilot.text: Inconsistent filter order: The filters are applied in a different order compared to querySplitting.ts (lines 296-298), which filters by !query.hide first, then by query.expr. For consistency, both files should use the same filter order.

Double interpolation issue: queries are interpolated in runShardSplitQuery, then interpolated again in runSplitQuery at line 299 (in querySplitting.ts). This can cause issues with adhoc filters being applied twice or nested variable references.

Test mock mutates input: The mock implementation mutates the input query object before returning it, but the real applyTemplateVariables method returns a new object using object spread. This discrepancy could hide bugs related to object mutation.

propel.text: Filtering by query.expr before applyTemplateVariables means targets whose expressions become empty after templating (e.g., $var resolving to empty selector) are still scheduled and sent to Loki, resulting in invalid/empty queries in shardQuerySplitting.ts line 52
Filtering by query.expr before applyTemplateVariables means targets whose expressions become empty after templating are still executed and can produce malformed requests in querySplitting.ts line 299

baz.text: runSplitQuery pre-interpolates with datasource.applyTemplateVariables, but datasource.runQuery calls DataSourceWithBackend.query which applies the same interpolation/addAdHocFilters again, causing ad hoc filters to be appended/escaped twice for every split request

runShardSplitQuery calls applyTemplateVariables before computing shards, but applyTemplateVariables leaves $__range/$__auto untouched, causing getSelectorForShardValues to see selectors with those literals, making fetchLabelValues use an invalid stream selector and silently breaking sharding for queries that reference $__range/$__auto in matchers

coderabbit.text: Line 112 expects applyTemplateVariables to be called 5 times, but runShardSplitQuery only calls it once per initial target (test has single target), so expectation should be 1 instead of 5

kg.text: In querySplitting.ts line 298: filter((query) => query.expr) is applied before applyTemplateVariables, checking the original expr instead of the interpolated one. If a template variable resolves to an empty string, the query should be filtered out but would be incorrectly included. Move the filter after the map to filter on interpolated expressions.

In shardQuerySplitting.ts line 50: filter((query) => query.expr) is applied before applyTemplateVariables, checking the original expr instead of the interpolated one. If a template variable resolves to an empty string, the query should be filtered out but would be incorrectly included. Move the filter after the map to filter on interpolated expressions.

devin.text: Double interpolation when shard splitting delegates to time splitting causes duplicate ad-hoc filters: runShardSplitQuery interpolates queries via applyTemplateVariables at shardQuerySplitting.ts:52, then passes already-interpolated targets into runSplitQuery at shardQuerySplitting.ts:163, where applyTemplateVariables is called again at querySplitting.ts:299, resulting in duplicate filter labels in queries sent to Loki

mra-nano.text: Loki interpolation API migration introduces injection risk through unsanitized interpolated values that may come from user controls, enabling potential LogQL injection or query manipulation
applyTemplateVariables mutates LokiQuery objects in place during runSplitQuery, risking cross-request contamination and hard-to-trace bugs when input objects are reused

Two parallel interpolation entry points (applyTemplateVariables in backend, interpolateVariablesInQueries in frontend UI) create API divergence with potentially different semantics for variable substitution and ad-hoc filters
Lack of input validation and escaping for interpolated values, missing unit tests for edge cases like quotes, braces, pipes, and backslashes
Tests in querySplitting.test.ts rely on exact string substitutions for interpolation, making them brittle to changes in interpolation logic
Shard-splitting tests lack coverage for edge cases such as empty or single shard scenarios and concurrent requests
Missing documentation describing the two interpolation methods, when each is used, how ad-hoc filters are handled, and security considerations

qodo-v2.text: Shard splitting applies applyTemplateVariables() multiple times per execution path (once in runShardSplitQuery(), again in runSplitQuery(), and again in DataSourceWithBackend.query()), causing unnecessary CPU overhead and potential filter duplication

cubic-dev.text: Missing type annotation causes TypeScript to infer the type as `undefined` instead of `number | undefined` (as declared in the `GrafanaConfig` interface). This prevents assigning a number to this property through type-checked code paths.

Returning the error here is correct for device-limit enforcement, but `tagDeviceUI` sets the local cache *before* calling `CreateOrUpdateDevice`. When the store returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached`, the device key is already cached, so subsequent requests within the 29-minute cache window will get a cache hit, return `nil`, and bypass the limit.

When the device limit is reached, new (previously unseen) anonymous devices receive `ErrDeviceLimitReached` from `CreateOrUpdateDevice`. If the upstream authentication handler treats this as a hard failure, anonymous access is completely blocked for new users rather than gracefully degrading (e.g., allowing access but skipping device tracking).

Race condition: the count check and the subsequent insert are not atomic. Multiple concurrent requests can each read a count below the limit, pass the check, and all proceed to insert—exceeding the device limit.
No validation for negative `device_limit` values. A negative value silently disables the limit (treated as unlimited) because the downstream check is `deviceLimit > 0`.

The count query in `CreateOrUpdateDevice` calls `time.Now().UTC()` twice (for `from` and `to` bounds), which can return slightly different instants. More critically, if the database stores timestamps in server-local time (common with MySQL/SQLite) while the code sends UTC values, the BETWEEN comparisons may not match the intended device window.

`rowsAffected == 0` does not necessarily mean the device limit was reached. The UPDATE could match zero rows because the device doesn't exist yet (new anonymous user), or because the device's `updated_at` falls outside the BETWEEN window. Returning `ErrDeviceLimitReached` for all these cases is misleading.

The query string is wrapped in `[]interface{}` and then splatted into `dbSession.Exec(args...)`. While this mirrors an existing pattern in `CreateOrUpdateDevice`, confirm that `Exec` correctly handles an `interface{}`-wrapped string as the SQL argument across all supported database dialects. Additionally, the `?` placeholders in the UPDATE query may not be automatically translated for PostgreSQL, which expects `$1, $2, ...`—unlike the dialect-specific queries in `CreateOrUpdateDevice`.

Test naming inconsistency: the other tests in this file follow the `TestIntegrationAnonStore_<Method>` convention. Consider renaming to `TestIntegrationAnonStore_BeyondDeviceLimit` or similar to keep the file consistent.

qodo-v2-2.text: Device limit cache bypass: AnonDeviceService.tagDeviceUI caches the device ID before persisting it to the database. After the first rejection with ErrDeviceLimitReached, subsequent requests for the same device skip persistence due to the cache, allowing anonymous auth to succeed and breaking device-limit enforcement for the local cache TTL (29 minutes). The cache SetDefault should be moved to after successful CreateOrUpdateDevice.

qodo-v22.text: AnonDeviceService.tagDeviceUI writes the device ID into localCache before persisting to DB and does not clear it on error; after a first ErrDeviceLimitReached, subsequent requests for the same device ID hit the cache and skip DB enforcement, allowing anonymous auth to succeed for ~29 minutes

AnonDBStore.CreateOrUpdateDevice enforces the device cap with a separate CountDevices query before the insert/update, so concurrent requests can both observe count < limit and insert new rows, exceeding the configured limit

qodo-extended.text: Device limit reached error (ErrDeviceLimitReached) has no dedicated HTTP/status mapping, causing clients to receive generic unauthenticated behavior instead of a clear 'device limit reached' response

TagDevice is now called synchronously instead of in a fire-and-forget goroutine, adding 1-2 database round-trips (CountDevices + CreateOrUpdateDevice) to every anonymous authentication response
Synchronous TagDevice call removes the panic recovery safety net that previously protected the auth path

cubic-v2.text: Propagating this error makes device-limit enforcement bypassable after the first rejected request because the device is cached before the store write runs (impl.go:147)

When the device limit is reached and a new (untracked) device tries to authenticate, `updateDevice` returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached` which propagates to `client.go`'s `Authenticate`, returning `nil` and blocking anonymous access entirely. This means the device limit acts as a hard cap on anonymous authentication rather than just on device tracking (database.go:116)

The count-then-insert pattern has a TOCTOU race condition. Under concurrent load, multiple goroutines can read `count < deviceLimit` at the same moment and all proceed to insert, exceeding the limit (database.go:110)

`rowsAffected == 0` does not necessarily indicate the device limit was reached — the device may simply not exist in the DB, or its `updated_at` may be outside the `BETWEEN` window. Need to distinguish between 'device not found' and actual limit-related condition (database.go:96)
Missing `device_limit` in the shipped `[auth.anonymous]` config files; the new setting cannot be overridden via `GF_AUTH_ANONYMOUS_DEVICE_LIMIT` and silently falls back to `0` (setting.go:1654)

bugbot.text: The contents of `category_fabricator.rb` and `embeddable_host_fabricator.rb` are swapped. The category fabricator file now contains the `embeddable_host` fabricator definition, while the embeddable_host fabricator file contains all the category fabricators (`Fabricator(:category)`, `:diff_category`, `:happy_category`, `:private_category`). This appears to be an accidental copy/paste or file operation error.

The `update` and `destroy` actions fetch a host using `EmbeddableHost.where(id: params[:id]).first` but don't verify the record exists before operating on it. If an invalid ID is provided, `host` will be `nil`, causing a `NoMethodError` when calling `host.destroy` or passing it to `save_host`.

The `record_for_host` method uses `where("lower(host) = ?", host)` which lowercases the database column but not the `host` parameter. If the referer URL contains uppercase letters (e.g., "http://EvilTrout.com/page"), the comparison fails because `uri.host` preserves case, causing legitimate embeds to be rejected.

The `before_validation` callback calls `self.host.sub!(...)` without checking if `host` is nil. If a user submits the form with a missing or nil host value, this raises `NoMethodError: undefined method 'sub!' for nil:NilClass` instead of returning a proper validation error.

The migration accesses `execute(...)[0]['id']` without checking if the query returned any rows. If no `embed_category` setting exists or it doesn't match any category name, the result set is empty, `[0]` returns `nil`, and `nil['id']` raises `NoMethodError`. This crashes the migration during deployment for users who haven't configured embedding.

The `delete` action calls `destroyRecord()` without a `.catch()` handler. If the server request fails (network error, permission denied, etc.), the error is silently swallowed and the user receives no feedback.

The plural `*_ids` hydration branch unconditionally deletes the original key (`delete obj[k]`) regardless of whether hydration succeeded, while the singular `*_id` branch only deletes the key if hydration was successful. This inconsistency means that if plural ID lookups fail, the original IDs are lost and replaced with an array potentially containing undefined values.

`record_for_host` compares against `uri.host` (no port), but the model allows saving `host` values with `:port`, so a stored `example.com:3000` would never match and will be treated as disallowed. Consider normalizing/removing the port (or including it in the lookup) so behavior stays consistent.

This migration inserts `h` via string interpolation, which can break on quotes and also bypasses the model's host normalization (scheme/path stripping), potentially leaving records that will never match `record_for_host`. Consider quoting and normalizing `h` before insertion so migrated allowlist entries remain effective.

Server-side `save_host` defaults blank `category_id` to uncategorized, but the client sets `host.category` from `categoryId` rather than the saved record's returned `category_id`. If `categoryId` is blank/undefined, the UI could show no category even though the server persisted one.

mesa.text: Potential crash if `self.host` is nil. The `sub!` method will raise NoMethodError on nil. Add a nil check: `self.host.sub!(/^https?:\/\//, '') if self.host.present?`

Case-insensitive host matching creates a security risk: without a unique index on `LOWER(host)`, duplicate hosts with different casing can be created (e.g., 'Example.com' and 'example.com'), leading to ambiguous authorization checks. Add a database unique index on `LOWER(host)` in the migration.

SQL Injection vulnerability: The variable `h` (host from site settings) is directly interpolated into SQL without escaping. If the site_settings value contains malicious SQL (e.g., `'); DROP TABLE embeddable_hosts; --`), this will execute arbitrary SQL. Use parameterized queries or proper escaping.

Category lookup query will fail if no matching category is found (returns empty result set), causing `[0]['id']` to raise an error. Add error handling: check if the query returns results before accessing `[0]`, and handle the case where no category is found.

Missing nil check before calling `destroy` in embeddable_hosts_controller. If no host with the given ID exists, `host` will be nil and this will raise NoMethodError. Add: `return render json: failed_json, status: :not_found unless host` before line 16.

Missing nil check in update action. If the embeddable host doesn't exist, `host` will be nil and calling `save_host(nil)` will likely cause errors. Add: `return render json: failed_json, status: :not_found unless host` after line 10.

If `eh` is nil (no embeddable host found for the URL), `eh.try(:category_id)` returns nil, and the topic will be created without a category. This is a breaking behavioral change from the previous implementation which always used `SiteSetting.embed_category`. Consider falling back to a default category or `SiteSetting.uncategorized_category_id` when no embeddable host is found.
