cubic-dev.text: `prisma` is used but never imported in packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts line 96. This will throw `ReferenceError: prisma is not defined` at runtime.

`credentialId` (the credential's database primary key) is passed as the `userId` parameter of `refreshOAuthTokens` in packages/app-store/zoho-bigin/lib/CalendarService.ts line 93, which forwards it to the sync endpoint as `calcomUserId`. The user's actual ID (`credential.userId`) should be passed instead.

When credential sharing is enabled, `refreshOAuthTokens` returns a native Fetch `Response` (no `.data` property) instead of an Axios response in packages/app-store/zoho-bigin/lib/CalendarService.ts line 85. The subsequent `tokenInfo.data.error` access will throw a `TypeError` at runtime.

Missing `!!` coercion in packages/lib/constants.ts line 104: `APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED` evaluates to the literal `CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY` string (a secret) instead of a boolean. If this constant is ever logged or serialized, the encryption key leaks.

`z.object()` strips unrecognized keys by default in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/parseRefreshTokenResponse.ts line 25. Since the schema only recognizes `access_token`, all other OAuth response fields (`refresh_token`, `expires_in`, `token_type`, etc.) are silently dropped from `.data`. This means `refresh_token` is always `undefined` and always replaced with the placeholder string `"refresh_token"`.

`[z.string().toString()]` is a computed property that evaluates to a fixed literal string in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/parseRefreshTokenResponse.ts line 8, not a dynamic key matcher. This creates a single key with a nonsensical name like `"ZodString"` instead of allowing additional properties through.

The raw `fetch` `Response` is returned without parsing in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/refreshOAuthTokens.ts line 15. Callers access properties like `.data`, `.expiresIn`, `.expiryDate` on the return value, which don't exist on a `Response` object. When credential sharing is enabled, this will cause runtime errors.

When `APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED` is true, `refreshOAuthTokens` returns a raw `fetch` `Response` object (not parsed JSON) in packages/app-store/hubspot/lib/CalendarService.ts line 177. Casting it to `HubspotToken` silently yields `undefined` for `.accessToken` and `.expiresIn`, resulting in `NaN` expiry dates persisted to the DB.

When `APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED` is true, `refreshOAuthTokens` returns a raw `fetch` Response (which has no `.data` property) in packages/app-store/zohocrm/lib/CalendarService.ts line 204, but this code immediately accesses `zohoCrmTokenInfo.data.error`. This will throw a `TypeError` at runtime.

When credential sharing is enabled, `refreshOAuthTokens` returns a native `fetch` `Response` (no `.data` property) in packages/app-store/googlecalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts line 86, but the next line accesses `res?.data` expecting a GaxiosResponse shape. This will cause a TypeError in the credential-sharing path.

`parseRefreshTokenResponse` returns a Zod `safeParse` result (`{ success, data }`) in packages/app-store/googlecalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts line 97, not the parsed value directly. Storing this as `key` corrupts the credential in the database—on next read `googleCredentialSchema.parse(credential.key)` will fail.

Webhook secret comparison is vulnerable to timing attacks in apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts line 25. Use `crypto.timingSafeEqual` for constant-time string comparison to prevent attackers from guessing the secret by measuring response times.

Calling `parseRefreshTokenResponse` when credential sync is enabled in packages/app-store/office365calendar/lib/CalendarService.ts line 263 replaces the real OAuth `refresh_token` with the literal placeholder string `"refresh_token"`, which is then spread over the existing credentials and saved to the DB.

The missing encryption key is silently replaced with a hardcoded empty string `""` in apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts line 58. If `CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY` is not set and the earlier `APP_CREDENTIAL_SHARING_ENABLED` guard is ever refactored away, this would attempt AES256 decryption with a zero-length key.

This parser call in packages/app-store/zoomvideo/lib/VideoApiAdapter.ts line 104 can return token data that does not match `zoomRefreshedTokenSchema` in credential-sharing mode, but the code still persists `refresh_token`/`expires_in` as if they were guaranteed. That can corrupt stored OAuth credentials.

The slug is now hardcoded in packages/app-store/zoho-bigin/api/add.ts line 17 instead of using the already-imported `appConfig.slug`. This creates an inconsistency with `callback.ts` in the same directory, which still uses `${appConfig.slug}`.

The `!parsedToken.success` check following the call in packages/app-store/zoomvideo/lib/VideoApiAdapter.ts line 104 is now dead code. `parseRefreshTokenResponse` already throws `new Error("Invalid refreshed tokens were returned")` internally when parsing fails, so it never returns an object with `success: false`.

`response.statusText` is unreliable in packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts line 86—HTTP/2 omits it and some Node.js fetch implementations return an empty string. Use `!response.ok` (checks status 200–299) to reliably detect failures.

This check is unreachable in packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts line 92. `parseRefreshTokenResponse` throws an error internally when parsing fails rather than returning `{ success: false }`. This block will never execute.

No error handling for the fetch response in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/refreshOAuthTokens.ts line 8. If `CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT` returns a non-2xx status, the error is silently swallowed and passed to callers as if it were valid token data.

Webhook handler does not validate HTTP method in apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts line 17. Should restrict to POST to prevent accidental triggers from GET requests (e.g., crawlers, browser prefetch).

`refreshOAuthTokens` can return the credential-sync response (which doesn't include Lark's `code`/`msg` fields) in packages/app-store/larkcalendar/lib/CalendarService.ts line 67. `handleLarkError` will treat that as an error and throw, so Lark token refresh will fail when syncing is enabled.

Neither `symmetricDecrypt` nor `JSON.parse` is wrapped in error handling in apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts line 57. If the encrypted payload is corrupted or the decrypted content is not valid JSON, this will throw an unhandled exception and return a 500 with a stack trace.

When credential syncing is enabled, refreshOAuthTokens returns a payload that is documented to contain only access_token/expiry_date in packages/app-store/webex/lib/VideoApiAdapter.ts line 62, but this adapter still validates against the full Webex refresh schema (refresh_token, refresh_token_expires_in). That mismatch causes token refresh to fail for synced credentials.

refreshOAuthTokens can return the credential-sync response (access_token + expiry_date) in packages/app-store/office365video/lib/VideoApiAdapter.ts line 61, but this path still treats it like a Microsoft refresh response. That will overwrite expiry_date to "now" and drop refresh_token when saving, so the credential becomes immediately expired and can't be refreshed.

Unnecessarily roundabout import path in packages/app-store/_utils/oauth/createOAuthAppCredential.ts line 6. Since this file is already inside `_utils/oauth/`, importing `../oauth/decodeOAuthState` navigates up to `_utils` then back down into `oauth`. The original `./decodeOAuthState` was simpler and correct.
`CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` is out of alphabetical order in turbo.json line 207 — it should appear before `CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY`, alongside the other `CALCOM_WEBHOOK_*` entries.

kodus.text: Multiple integrations (Google Calendar, Webex, Zoho Bigin) experience runtime errors due to inconsistent return types from the `refreshOAuthTokens` utility. Some callers expect an axios-style response with a `.data` property, while others expect a raw `fetch` Response object, causing TypeErrors when accessing properties on the wrong type.

Error handling in token refresh operations is inconsistent across integrations. Hubspot implementation swallows errors silently in the catch block without re-throwing, preventing proper error propagation to callers.
Salesforce integration bypasses the centralized `refreshOAuthTokens` utility entirely, missing out on critical functionality like credential syncing.
Zoho Bigin redirect URI is hardcoded as 'zoho-bigin' instead of using `appConfig.slug`, breaking code reusability and creating issues when this code is used as a template for new integrations.
Feature flags don't properly validate all required environment variables before enabling functionality.

Webhook stores decrypted credentials in plaintext directly in the database instead of re-encrypting them with `CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY`, creating a critical security vulnerability where sensitive tokens and keys are stored unencrypted at rest.

`minimumTokenResponseSchema` contains a conflicting index signature `[z.string().toString()]: z.number()` that requires all properties including `access_token` to be numbers, conflicting with `access_token: z.string()` and causing parsing to always fail.

The `parseRefreshTokenResponse` function injects a static placeholder string `"refresh_token"` when the refresh_token field is missing, which corrupts credentials for integrations that don't explicitly preserve the old token and causes permanent OAuth failures.

When credential-sharing is enabled, `refreshOAuthTokens` returns a raw `fetch` Response object without a `.data` property, but callers in zoho-bigin, zohocrm, googlecalendar, and hubspot immediately access `.data`, resulting in `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined`.
Hubspot integration casts the Response object directly to `HubspotToken` and reads `.expiresIn` from it, producing `NaN` in the expiry calculation and corrupting stored credentials.

qodo-extended-summary.text: scheduleEmailReminders selects reminders to cancel without filtering to EMAIL or ensuring referenceId is non-null, then sends reminder.referenceId to SendGrid as batch_id. This can attempt to cancel non-email reminders (or any reminder missing a referenceId), causing SendGrid request failures and leaving reminders uncancelled.

In deleteScheduledEmailReminder, when immediateDelete=true and referenceId is present, the function sends a cancel request to SendGrid but returns without deleting the workflowReminder DB record or marking it as cancelled=true. These orphaned records won't be caught by the CRON's cancelled-reminder query (which filters on cancelled: true) and will linger in the database until their scheduledDate passes.

In handleCancelBooking.ts, forEach is used to invoke async deleteScheduledEmailReminder and deleteScheduledSMSReminder without awaiting the returned promises. If the DB update to set cancelled=true fails (transient DB error), the error is silently swallowed by the function's internal catch, the caller never knows, and the reminder stays uncancelled — meaning the email will still be sent for a cancelled booking.

In scheduleEmailReminders.ts, the entire cancellation loop is wrapped in a single try/catch. If any client.request() call throws (e.g., network error, duplicate cancel), the catch block fires and Promise.all(workflowRemindersToDelete) is never awaited, so all previously accumulated Prisma delete promises are silently dropped. Reminders whose SendGrid cancellation already succeeded will not be deleted from the DB and will be retried on the next CRON run.

The unscheduledReminders query in scheduleEmailReminders.ts fetches reminders with scheduled=false but does NOT exclude reminders with cancelled=true. A reminder that was created but not yet scheduled (e.g., scheduled date is >72 hours away) and then cancelled will be picked up by this query and scheduled via SendGrid, sending an unwanted email.

copilot.text: Direct mutation of props: The value prop is being mutated directly with 'value = value || []'. This is generally considered bad practice in React as it mutates the input parameter. Consider using a local variable or handling the null/undefined case in the destructuring or with a more explicit check.

Inconsistent naming: The action id is "add_members" but the feature is about adding guests, not members. This creates confusion with the data-testid in AddGuestsDialog which is also "add_members". Consider using "add_guests" for consistency with the rest of the feature naming.

Critical authorization bug: The logic uses AND (&&) when it should use OR (||). Currently, a user must be BOTH an admin AND an owner to pass this check, which is impossible since a user can only have one role. This means team admins who are not owners cannot add guests. The condition should be checking if the user is an admin OR an owner.

The isInvalidEmail error state persists even after the user modifies the email values. Once set to true, it remains visible until the dialog is closed or canceled. Consider resetting this error state when the user modifies the multiEmailValue to provide better UX feedback.

Case sensitivity issue in email comparison: The newGuests array contains email addresses as provided by the user, while attendee.email comes from the database. If there's any case difference, the comparison will fail and new guests will receive AttendeeAddGuestsEmail instead of AttendeeScheduledEmail. Consider using case-insensitive comparison by converting both emails to lowercase.

Case sensitivity issue in duplicate check: The comparison between guest and attendee.email is case-sensitive. This means if an attendee exists with email "User@Example.com" and someone tries to add "user@example.com", it will be treated as a different email and allowed through. Email addresses should be compared in a case-insensitive manner.

Using array index as React key is an anti-pattern, especially when items can be added or removed. When an item is removed, React may incorrectly match components to their state, leading to bugs. Consider using a unique identifier for each email field instead of the array index.

Error swallowing without proper logging: The catch block only logs a generic message without logging the actual error details. This makes debugging difficult if email sending fails. Consider logging the error object: console.error("Error sending AddGuestsEmails", err)

Case sensitivity bug: The blacklistedGuestEmails are converted to lowercase, but the guest emails being compared are not. This means the blacklist check will fail if a guest email has uppercase characters. The guest email should be converted to lowercase before the comparison.

The validation logic does not filter out empty strings before checking. When the dialog starts with multiEmailValue set to [""], if a user clicks "Add" immediately, it will attempt to validate an array containing an empty string. The z.string().email() validator will correctly reject empty strings, but it would be cleaner to filter out empty strings before validation to avoid misleading error messages.

Missing eventTypeMetadata parameter: The sendAddGuestsEmails function does not check eventTypeDisableHostEmail and eventTypeDisableAttendeeEmail flags like other similar email functions in this file (e.g., sendLocationChangeEmails). This means emails will be sent even if the event type has these email notifications disabled, which is inconsistent with the rest of the codebase.

qodo-v22.text: isTeamAdminOrOwner uses logical AND between isTeamAdmin and isTeamOwner, effectively restricting access to team OWNERS only and blocking legitimate team ADMINs from adding guests

qodo-extended.text: Team admins blocked: addGuestsHandler requires a user to be BOTH team admin and team owner to pass the team permission check, so accepted ADMIN members (non-OWNER) cannot add guests unless they are also the booking organizer or an attendee. isTeamAdminOrOwner uses && between isTeamAdmin and isTeamOwner, making the team-path effectively OWNER-only.

Duplicate guests accepted: The backend schema for addGuests accepts duplicate emails in the guests array, and addGuestsHandler does not dedupe within the request, so a crafted request can create multiple attendee rows for the same email and cause repeated calendar/email updates.

Email disable flags ignored: sendAddGuestsEmails always sends organizer and attendee emails and does not honor disableStandardEmails metadata checks used by other booking email flows, so events configured to suppress standard emails will still send 'guests added' notifications.
Email errors hidden: addGuestsHandler swallows sendAddGuestsEmails failures with a console.log that drops the underlying error, making production failures hard to detect and debug.

Blacklist bypass via case mismatch: Guest emails are compared against the lowercased blacklist without lowercasing the guest email first, so submitting Blocked@Email.COM bypasses the blacklist entry blocked@email.com.

Wrong guest list for emails: The original unfiltered guests input is passed to sendAddGuestsEmails instead of the filtered uniqueGuests. In email-manager.ts, newGuests.includes(attendee.email) determines which email template each attendee receives, so passing unfiltered input causes existing attendees whose emails were re-submitted to incorrectly receive AttendeeScheduledEmail instead of AttendeeAddGuestsEmail.

Unbounded guests array enables spam: The guests array in the input schema lacks a maximum length constraint. An attacker can submit an arbitrarily large array of emails, causing the server to send mass spam and potentially causing resource exhaustion.

Adding guests bypasses seat limits: Adding guests does not check the event's seatsPerTimeSlot limit. Attendees can add guests and exceed the event's maximum capacity, bypassing overbooking constraints.

cubic-dev.text: The first test is missing the backup code dialog dismissal that was added to the second test. After `fillOtp` enables 2FA, the backup codes dialog now appears and must be closed before the switch state can be verified. Without dismissing it, this test either fails or silently passes due to the Promise-is-truthy bug noted in the FIXME (`.isChecked()` returns a `Promise<boolean>`, which is always truthy when passed to `expect().toBeTruthy()`).

Backup codes are stored with reversible symmetric encryption instead of being hashed. Since backup codes only need to be verified (not recovered), each code should be individually hashed (e.g., with `bcrypt` or salted SHA-256). With the current approach, a leaked `CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY` exposes every user's backup codes at once.

Race condition: the backup code is read, validated, then invalidated in separate non-atomic steps. Two concurrent requests can both read the same backup codes before either writes the update, allowing the same one-time code to be used more than once. Wrap the read-validate-update in a Prisma transaction (or use an atomic `updateMany` with a WHERE clause that includes the current encrypted value) to prevent concurrent reuse.

Password verification can be bypassed when a backup code is used alongside a non-empty `totpCode`. The password check is gated on `!credentials.totpCode`, so sending a dummy `totpCode` value (e.g., `"x"`) skips password verification, then the backup code branch validates the backup code alone. Since backup codes are static and long-lived (unlike TOTP), this meaningfully increases the risk of account takeover if backup codes are leaked.

Adding `tabIndex={-1}` removes the show/hide password toggle from the keyboard tab order, making it inaccessible to keyboard-only users. This is a shared component, so it affects all password fields site-wide.

The exported function is named `TwoFactor`, which conflicts with the existing `TwoFactor` component in `TwoFactor.tsx` in the same directory. Rename it to `BackupCode` to match the file name and the component's actual purpose.

`req.body.backupCode` is not validated to be a string before calling `.replaceAll()`. A non-string truthy value (e.g., `123`, `true`) will throw a `TypeError` at runtime, producing an unhandled 500 instead of a clear 400 error.
Nested ternary without parentheses is hard to parse. Adding explicit parentheses or refactoring to a helper/early-return would make the intent immediately clear and avoid potential misinterpretation.

The Blob URL created via `URL.createObjectURL` is never revoked on component unmount. Add a `useEffect` cleanup to call `URL.revokeObjectURL(backupCodesUrl)` when the component unmounts, to avoid a memory leak.
`useState([])` is inferred as `never[]` by TypeScript. This makes all subsequent operations on `backupCodes` type-unsafe. Add a type parameter: `useState<string[]>([])`.

`PasswordField` manages its own `type` internally to support the show/hide toggle. Passing `type="password"` here overrides that behavior (since `{...props}` is spread after the internal `type` in `PasswordField`), breaking the toggle.

Add `type="button"` to prevent this button from triggering form submission. Without an explicit type, HTML buttons inside forms default to `type="submit"`, so clicking "Lost access" / "Go back" would invoke `handleDisable` instead of just toggling the view.

Backup code comparison is case-sensitive. If the generated codes contain letters, a user entering them in different case will be incorrectly rejected. Normalize both sides to the same case before comparing.
Reset state should also clear backup codes and revoke the existing object URL; otherwise the old download link can persist and the blob URL is never cleaned up when the modal is closed.

mesa.text: Backup code validation uses indexOf which performs simple string equality checks, making it vulnerable to timing attacks where an attacker could infer information about valid codes based on comparison time

Backup code validation and invalidation flow has a race condition vulnerability where two concurrent login attempts using the same backup code could both succeed before the update at line 149-156 persists
JSON.parse of decrypted backup codes at line 139 lacks error handling - corrupted, malformed, or legacy schema data will throw an unhandled exception
Backup codes generated using crypto.randomBytes(5) produce only 40 bits of entropy per code, and no rate limiting is applied to backup code login attempts to prevent brute force attacks
Missing comprehensive E2E tests for backup code authentication covering successful login, rejection when reusing codes, disabling 2FA with backup codes, and error handling for invalid codes
JSON.parse at line 58 in disable.ts lacks error handling for corrupted or malformed encrypted backup code data

claude-code.text: Dismissing the dialog (via Escape key or clicking outside) during the backup codes step bypasses the `onEnable` callback. Since `onEnable` is the only path that calls `utils.viewer.me.invalidate()`, the UI will show 2FA as disabled even though it was successfully enabled on the server.

The first e2e test never interacts with the new backup codes screen after OTP verification. After `fillOtp` succeeds, the modal transitions to `SetupStep.DisplayBackupCodes` instead of calling `onEnable()`, but the test skips straight to checking the two-factor-switch without clicking the close button.

The assertion on line 53 of the first e2e test uses `expect(page.locator(...).isChecked()).toBeTruthy()` which evaluates the Promise object itself (always truthy) rather than the resolved boolean, causing the assertion to pass regardless of actual switch state.

The `PasswordField` on line 167 has an explicit `type="password"` prop that breaks the show/hide password toggle. The explicit prop overrides the internal `isPasswordVisible` state management because `{...props}` is spread after the internal `type` prop in `InputField`.

`BackupCode.tsx` line 7 exports `function TwoFactor` instead of `function BackupCode`. This causes the wrong function name to appear in React DevTools and error stack traces, creating confusion since a real `TwoFactor` component already exists in `TwoFactor.tsx`.

cubic-dev.text: Changing `getCalendar` to `async` breaks the synchronous caller `getCalendarCredentials` in `CalendarManager.ts`. It calls `getCalendar(credential)` inside a `.flatMap()` callback without awaiting, so the `calendar` field will be an unresolved `Promise` instead of the actual `Calendar` instance. This will silently break calendar operations at runtime. The caller needs to be converted to use `Promise.all` or an equivalent async pattern.

The `await getCalendar(...)` call (and subsequent async operations) inside `forEach` in wipemycalother/lib/reschedule.ts are not caught by the surrounding try/catch. Because `forEach` doesn't await the async callbacks, any rejection from the awaited import or deletion will produce an unhandled promise rejection instead of being logged. Switching to `await Promise.all(...map(...))` will restore the try/catch's ability to handle these errors.

Using `async` callbacks inside `forEach` in packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/bookings.tsx results in fire-and-forget promises — `forEach` does not await the returned promises. Calendar/video deletions will run concurrently with the rest of the function (e.g., sending emails), and any rejection will be an unhandled promise rejection rather than a caught error. Replace `forEach` with `Promise.all` + `map` so deletions are properly awaited before proceeding.

The `async` callback inside `.forEach()` in packages/features/bookings/lib/handleCancelBooking.ts is fire-and-forget — `forEach` discards the returned promises, so `await getCalendar(credential)`, `await calendar?.deleteEvent(...)`, and the subsequent `apiDeletes.push(deletedEvent)` all execute after the outer function has already moved past this block. Calendar events for recurring bookings will silently fail to be deleted. Convert this to a `for...of` loop.

`async` callbacks inside `forEach` in packages/app-store/vital/lib/reschedule.ts are fire-and-forget — the returned promises are never awaited, so the `try/catch` block cannot catch rejections and the deletions race with the subsequent email send. Use `Promise.all` with `.map` (or a `for...of` loop) to properly await each operation.

Dynamic `import()` calls in packages/app-store/index.ts should be wrapped with error handling (e.g., `.catch()`) to gracefully handle module load failures. Currently, if any app module fails to load, the rejected promise will propagate as an unhandled error to any consumer that awaits it.

All `import()` calls in packages/app-store/index.ts execute eagerly at module load time since they're direct expressions in the object literal—this doesn't achieve lazy loading. Every property is now a `Promise<module>` that fires immediately, loading all 28 modules in parallel as soon as this file is imported. If the goal is to defer loading until a specific app is actually needed, wrap them in thunks.

The awaited `getCalendar` call in packages/features/bookings/lib/handleNewBooking.ts should be wrapped in a try/catch (or otherwise handle the rejection) so that a failure for one credential doesn't abort processing of the remaining references and the subsequent booking-status update. Without error handling, a single failed async import leaves the booking in an inconsistent state: some integrations deleted, others untouched, and the status never set to `CANCELLED`.

The `await getCalendar(credential)` call in packages/core/CalendarManager.ts is not wrapped in a try/catch. Since `getCalendar` now performs a dynamic `await appStore[...]` import, a failed import will throw an unhandled exception that propagates to the caller. Consider wrapping this in try/catch to handle import failures gracefully.

The awaited dynamic import in packages/lib/payment/handlePayment.ts should be wrapped in a try/catch so that a failed module load produces a meaningful error message (e.g., which `dirName` failed) instead of an opaque rejection propagating to the caller.

The awaited dynamic import in packages/lib/payment/deletePayment.ts should be wrapped in a try/catch. If `dirName` is invalid or the module fails to load, the `await` will throw and bypass the existing falsy-check guard on the next line, propagating an unhandled error to callers.

The awaited dynamic import (`await appStore[...]`) in packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/bookings.tsx can reject if the `dirName` is invalid or the module fails to load, and there is no error handling around it. The existing `if (!(paymentApp && ...))` guard only handles a resolved-but-incomplete value, not a thrown error. Wrap this in a try-catch to handle import failures gracefully instead of crashing the request.

The awaited dynamic import `await appStore[...]` in packages/features/bookings/lib/handleCancelBooking.ts has no error handling. If the import fails (invalid `dirName`, module load error), it throws an unhandled exception that aborts the entire cancellation handler. Wrap in try/catch to handle import failures gracefully.

The awaited dynamic import in packages/app-store/_utils/getCalendar.ts should be wrapped in a try-catch so that a failed module load is handled gracefully (e.g., returning `null`) instead of throwing an unhandled rejection up to every caller.
